This bead should produce the ranked frontier for what comes next, including approved cards, rejected cards, and speculative ideas that remain below threshold. Future agents should be able to pick up from this bead without reopening the full design debate.
","created_at":"2026-03-23T20:47:15Z"},{"id":2668,"issue_id":"bd-db300.5.8.5","author":"ubuntu","text":"## E8.5 Advanced Card Verdicts and Ranked Frontier

Based on E8.1 decision record (settled from A7.1/A7.2/A7.3 evidence).

### Approved — Start Now

| Rank | Card | Target | EV | Verdict | Rationale |
|------|------|--------|-----|---------|-----------|
| **1** | **C8.6** | 8t flusher batch churn | 3.0 | **GO** | Narrowest scope, lowest risk. Flusher service time (30µs avg, 102µs spike) is the multiplier on waiter queue depth. Shrinking it proportionally reduces the 45% queueing bucket. |
| **2** | **D5.2** | c1 fused prepared-DML | 4.0 | **GO** | Independent code surface (connection.rs), parallel-safe with C8.6. Attacks the 70% ceremony-dominated c1 cell. Highest absolute EV. |

### Approved — Conditional (after C8.6 evidence)

| Rank | Card | Target | EV | Verdict | Gate |
|------|------|--------|-----|---------|------|
| **3** | **C8.4** | 8t/16t parallel WAL commit | 5.0 | **CONDITIONAL GO** | Start only after C8.6 lands AND post-C8.6 phase pack shows flusher service is no longer >50% of phase_B. If queueing is still dominant after C8.6, C8.4 is the only structural fix. |

### Deferred — Below Threshold

| Card | Target | EV | Verdict | Re-entry condition |
|------|--------|-----|---------|-------------------|
| C8.3 | 16t convoy audit | 1.5 | **DEFER** | Re-evaluate after C8.4 changes the flusher/waiter shape. The 22% timeout-wakes may vanish once the single-flusher monopoly is broken. |
| E3.3 | Snapshot publication | 1.5 | **DEFER** | Partially implemented. Pager Mutex is 5% of c1 cost. Complete after D5.2 proves the ceremony-elimination thesis. |
| DRO (bd-3t52f) | SSI abort policy | 1.0 | **DEFER** | Conflicts are <10% of cost in every red cell. Re-evaluate only if C8.4 creates conflict-dominated cells at higher concurrency. |
| C8.5 | Arrival-wait policy | 0.8 | **DEFER** | arrival_wait is 15µs (8t) and 2µs (16t). Not the dominant cost. Changing the heuristic before reducing flusher service risks making spikes worse. |

### Rejected

| Card | Verdict | Reason |
|------|---------|--------|
| HTM (bd-77l3t) | **REJECT** | Workspace forbids unsafe code. Flat combiner lock is <5% of any red cell. Design document preserved for reference. |
| io_uring | **REJECT** | wal_sync=0µs across all measured iterations. Raw I/O is not the bottleneck. |
| MVCC-conflict-first | **REJECT (anti-goal)** | MVCC/SW ratio is 0.924x at c1. Conflict work helps c8+/hot-page but is irrelevant to the two worst red cells (c1 ceremony, 8t queueing). |

### Frontier Summary

```
ACTIVE:  C8.6 ──┐
                 ├──▶ measure ──▶ C8.4 (if queueing still dominant)
ACTIVE:  D5.2 ──┘
WAITING: C8.3 (after C8.4)
WAITING: E3.3 (after D5.2)
WAITING: DRO  (after C8.4 if conflicts emerge)
DEAD:    HTM, io_uring, MVCC-conflict-first
```

### Note on incomplete dependencies
E8.2 (publication primitives), E8.3 (segmented durability), E8.4 (adaptive batching safety), C8.8 (post-step reruns), and D5.7 (c1 rerun) are still open. This verdict is based on E8.1's evidence-settled cards. If E8.2-E8.4 produce new candidates that pass the go/no-go rubric, they enter the frontier at their scored rank. The current frontier does not block on those beads — C8.6 and D5.2 are already approved and actionable.

 {"id":"bd-db300.6","title":"Track F: async pre-JIT and compiled-cache strategy","description":"## Goal
This track handles the user's pre-JIT hypothesis rigorously instead of hand-waving. The current JIT scaffold was pure overhead because it hashed programs and touched shared state on the hot path without ever producing a compiled fast path. A future compiled cache is only justified if statement repetition and compile amortization data show that interpreter-first plus asynchronous compilation can deliver net wins without contaminating latency.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The project has a clear go/no-go decision on background pre-JIT.
- Any implementation keeps compile work off the execute path.
- Correctness, cache invalidation, and latency effects are explicitly tested.
- This bead set explicitly includes comprehensive unit-test, end-to-end, and structured-logging obligations rather than treating verification as implied follow-up work.","design":"- Decide based on measured reuse patterns, not intuition.
- Keep the hot path interpreter-first and non-blocking.
- If a compiled cache exists, compilation must happen off-path with clear invalidation and size controls.","acceptance_criteria":"- The project has a clear go/no-go decision on background pre-JIT.
- Any implementation keeps compile work off the execute path.
- Correctness, cache invalidation, and latency effects are explicitly tested.
- This bead set explicitly includes comprehensive unit-test, end-to-end, and structured-logging obligations rather than treating verification as implied follow-up work.","notes":"This track is intentionally lower priority than the commit-path and synchronization tracks. It is a leverage bet, not the current root cause, and it must stay evidence-driven.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-10T04:47:12.443426654Z","created_by":"codex","updated_at":"2026-03-10T18:51:16.166627157Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db300-program","db300-track-f","optimization","perf","prepared-statements","program","track-f","vdbe"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-db300.6.1","title":"F1: measure repeat-statement locality and compile amortization","description":"## Goal
Measure statement repetition, locality, and compile amortization so the project can decide whether an async pre-JIT path is actually worth building. This bead exists to keep the JIT idea evidence-driven instead of letting it become an attractive but unproven side quest, and it must account for whether reuse is lane-local or cross-core enough to justify background compilation without hurting front-end locality.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. The workload data shows how often statements repeat, what the reuse distance looks like, how much locality is lane-local versus cross-core or cross-node, and what compile cost would need to be amortized.
2. First-hit latency, warmed-hit latency, and any measurable background-compile interference on hot benchmark cells are captured.
3. The result is a clear go or no-go recommendation for background compilation, including whether any compiled cache should be per-lane, shared, or rejected.
4. The recommendation is grounded in the real benchmark fixtures and at least one nontrivial placement profile, not a toy microbenchmark alone.
5. The scripts, harness changes, or reports in this bead emit structured logs and artifact manifests rich enough to reproduce results without reconstructing the workflow by hand.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The workload data shows how often statements repeat, what the reuse distance looks like, how much locality is lane-local versus cross-core or cross-node, and what compile cost would need to be amortized.
2. First-hit latency, warmed-hit latency, and any measurable background-compile interference on hot benchmark cells are captured.
3. The result is a clear go or no-go recommendation for background compilation, including whether any compiled cache should be per-lane, shared, or rejected.
4. The recommendation is grounded in the real benchmark fixtures and at least one nontrivial placement profile, not a toy microbenchmark alone.
5. The scripts, harness changes, or reports in this bead emit structured logs and artifact manifests rich enough to reproduce results without reconstructing the workflow by hand.","notes":"Because the current JIT scaffold was all overhead and no compiled fast path, this bead must set a high bar for adding complexity back in.  {"id":"bd-db300.6.2","title":"F2: design an interpreter-first async compile queue with zero execute-path blocking","description":"## Goal
Design the interpreter-first async compile queue and compiled-cache lifecycle so compile work can happen off the execute path without polluting latency-sensitive lanes, instruction cache, LLC locality, or TLB behavior. This bead should turn the hybrid idea into a concrete design with compile-debt budgeting, duplicate suppression, admission control, and helper-core placement instead of a vague background-JIT concept.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. The design states when a statement is eligible for background compilation and what locality or reuse evidence justifies spending cycles on it.
2. Compilation never blocks the current execution of the statement and runs under an explicit bounded queue or compile-debt budget tied to the many-core scheduling policy.
3. Cache fill, invalidation, duplicate-compile suppression, eviction, and concurrency behavior are explicit.
4. The design explains how compile work is placed to avoid harming foreground lanes, including whether it may share a core, LLC domain, or NUMA node with latency-sensitive execution.
5. The design defines metrics for first-hit latency, warmed-hit latency, compile success rate, queue depth, and background interference.
6. The resulting design or decision record includes an explicit verification plan describing the unit tests, end-to-end scenarios, and logging artifacts required for follow-on implementation.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The design states when a statement is eligible for background compilation and what locality or reuse evidence justifies spending cycles on it.
2. Compilation never blocks the current execution of the statement and runs under an explicit bounded queue or compile-debt budget tied to the many-core scheduling policy.
3. Cache fill, invalidation, duplicate-compile suppression, eviction, and concurrency behavior are explicit.
4. The design explains how compile work is placed to avoid harming foreground lanes, including whether it may share a core, LLC domain, or NUMA node with latency-sensitive execution.
5. The design defines metrics for first-hit latency, warmed-hit latency, compile success rate, queue depth, and background interference.
6. The resulting design or decision record includes an explicit verification plan describing the unit tests, end-to-end scenarios, and logging artifacts required for follow-on implementation.","notes":"This design should assume the interpreter remains the fallback and initial execution path. The compiled cache is an optimization layer, not a new mandatory dependency, and it must not quietly trade away responsiveness for possible future wins.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-10T05:07:38.715479949Z","created_by":"codex","updated_at":"2026-03-10T18:51:16.570381115Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","db300-program","db300-track-f","parallelism","prepared-statements","track-f","vdbe"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.5.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.5.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-dvo4","title":"Run Windows CI interop execution for WindowsVfs","description":"Execute bd-3bkm Windows-only verification on a native Windows runner: run WindowsVfs unit suite and test_e2e_windowsvfs_c_sqlite_interop end-to-end against C sqlite3. This follow-up exists because Linux hosts can only do cross-target compile/lint, not execute Win32 tests.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:32:47.715048630Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T00:53:00.524724020Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T00:53:00.524701228Z","close_reason":"Native Windows workflow executed successfully with full lane green, including test_e2e_windowsvfs_c_sqlite_interop (GH run 21847000388).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":1739,"issue_id":"bd-dvo4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Lock-byte semantics verified under real Win32 runtime.
- [ ] CreateFileMapping semantics verified under real Win32 runtime.
- [ ] Logs and artifacts captured from Windows CI execution.

## Unit Test Requirements
- test_windows_ci_unit_suite: All WindowsVfs unit tests pass on Windows.
- test_windows_ci_lock_byte: Lock-byte range operations verified on NTFS.
- test_windows_ci_file_mapping: CreateFileMapping SHM operations verified.

## E2E Test
- test_e2e_windowsvfs_ci_interop: Full interop test against C sqlite3 on Windows.

## Logging Requirements
- DEBUG: emit Win32 API call parameters and return codes.
- INFO: emit test suite results and interop comparison summary.
- WARN: emit platform-specific behavior differences.
- ERROR: emit Win32 API failures with GetLastError codes.

-{"id":"bd-dwyer","title":"TJ: Track J unit + e2e tests — lazy MemDB dirty tracking correctness, read-after-write through B-tree fallback","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"test","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:48:23.011018592Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-15T04:07:41.471050271Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-dwyer","depends_on_id":"bd-g595c","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:48:23.620511040Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3403,"issue_id":"bd-dwyer","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Unit Tests (crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs + crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs)
1. test_lazy_dirty_flag_set_on_insert — INSERT sets dirty flag for table, does NOT call sync_storage
2. test_lazy_dirty_read_from_btree — SELECT on dirty table reads from B-tree, returns correct data
3. test_lazy_dirty_clear_on_commit — COMMIT clears all dirty flags
4. test_lazy_dirty_rollback — ROLLBACK clears dirty flags and restores pre-txn state
5. test_lazy_dirty_multiple_tables — INSERT into table A, SELECT from table B (not dirty) uses MemDB fast path
6. test_lazy_dirty_update_after_insert — INSERT then UPDATE same row within same txn, both visible via B-tree fallback
7. test_lazy_dirty_delete_after_insert — INSERT then DELETE same row, SELECT returns empty

## E2E Tests
8. test_lazy_memdb_insert_10k_correctness — 10K inserts, verify all rows readable via SELECT, oracle comparison
9. test_lazy_memdb_mixed_dml — INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/SELECT interleaved, oracle comparison
10. test_lazy_memdb_cross_statement_visibility — multi-statement txn, each statement sees prior writes

## Logging
- dirty_flag_set_count, dirty_flag_clear_count, btree_fallback_read_count, memdb_fast_path_count per test

## CRITICAL: This optimization MUST NOT break read-after-write visibility
If any test fails, the lazy tracking is incorrect and must be reverted to eager sync.
","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:48:23Z"},{"id":3529,"issue_id":"bd-dwyer","author":"ubuntu","text":"Implemented a focused Track J test slice for lazy MemDB dirty tracking.

What changed:
- Added a VDBE unit test in `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs` that writes to one table, scans a clean table, then reads the dirty table in the same statement. It verifies clean-table visibility is preserved, dirty rows remain visible through the storage-backed B-tree fallback, and only the written root page is marked dirty.
- Added a passing multi-table e2e test in `crates/fsqlite-e2e/tests/correctness_transactions.rs` that exercises explicit-transaction visibility across a dirty table and a clean table after a read boundary.
- Added an ignored e2e regression test documenting the currently failing path: after a clean-table read boundary, a later insert into the dirty table is not yet visible to `COUNT(*)` through the lazy MemDB compatibility path.

Validation:
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p fsqlite-vdbe test_lazy_dirty_clean_table_scan_then_dirty_table_fallback -- --nocapture` ✅
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p fsqlite-e2e --test correctness_transactions test_lazy_memdb_multi_table_clean_read_then_dirty_visibility -- --nocapture` ✅
- `cargo check --workspace --all-targets` ✅
- `cargo fmt --check` ✅
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` ❌ unrelated existing failures:
  - `crates/fsqlite-pager/src/pager.rs:1980` `clippy::cloned_instead_of_copied`
  - `crates/fsqlite-pager/src/pager.rs:444` `clippy::significant_drop_in_scrutinee`
  - `crates/fsqlite-types/src/record.rs:1430` `clippy::useless_conversion`

Constraints held:
- `concurrent_mode_default` untouched
- no `unsafe`
- no Tokio/asupersync violations
- manual edits only
","created_at":"2026-04-10T19:34:46Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-e1hty","title":"EPIC: C-SQLite Parity Architecture — 5 Structural Changes to Match C SQLite Single-Thread DML Speed","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:30:02.540503943Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T18:34:02.604628907Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-e1hty","depends_on_id":"bd-1bzmw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:31:30.187515206Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-e1hty","depends_on_id":"bd-25zqf","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:32:52.621307700Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-e1hty","depends_on_id":"bd-c1fst","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:33:29.579629715Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-e1hty","depends_on_id":"bd-i9sov","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:32:17.260552896Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-e1hty","depends_on_id":"bd-u6u1q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:31:36.047395969Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-e1hty","depends_on_id":"bd-z4zl7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:34:02.603763434Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3431,"issue_id":"bd-e1hty","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Context & Evidence

Benchmark (2026-04-01) shows FrankenSQLite 3-5x slower than C SQLite on single-thread DML despite 10 P0 optimization beads already landed. Deep analysis of C SQLite's source code (legacy_sqlite_code/sqlite/src/) reveals 5 specific architectural patterns that make C SQLite fast — patterns that FrankenSQLite's Rust abstractions currently prevent.

### The 5 Gaps (from C SQLite source analysis)

| # | C SQLite Pattern | FrankenSQLite Today | Per-Row Cost Delta | Speedup |
|---|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|---------|
| 1 | Flat array hash, no locks (pcache1.c:1000) | 128 Mutex<HashMap> shards (page_cache.rs:532) | +35 cycles/page | 4.5x |
| 2 | MakeRecord into pre-alloc buffer (vdbe.c OP_MakeRecord) | Vec<u8> alloc per record, 3 passes (record.rs:844) | +100 cycles/row | 2-3x |
| 3 | Mem union, zero refcounting (vdbeaux.c) | SqliteValue with Arc/SmallText (value.rs) | +50 cycles/row | 1.5-2x |
| 4 | BTREE_APPEND O(1) insert (btree.c:9394) | Full seek in some paths despite G1 fix | +200 cycles/seq row | 2-5x |
| 5 | sqlite3PagerWrite 1-cycle dirty check (pager.c:6234) | Group commit Mutex+Condvar (pager.rs:38) | +100-500 cycles/row | 2-10x |

### Current Benchmark Numbers
- INSERT single txn (small 3-col): 3.34x slower than C SQLite
- INSERT autocommit 10K: 30.54x slower
- UPDATE 100K: 7.86x slower
- DELETE 100K: 5.39x slower
- Concurrent 8 writers: 6.72x FASTER (MVCC advantage — MUST NOT regress)

### Target
- INSERT single txn: ≤ 1.5x C SQLite (from 3.34x)
- INSERT autocommit: ≤ 5.0x (from 30.54x)
- UPDATE: ≤ 2.0x (from 7.86x)
- DELETE: ≤ 2.0x (from 5.39x)
- Concurrent writers: ≥ 6.0x faster (no regression)

### Strategy: 5 tracks (Q-U) implementing C-SQLite-equivalent patterns in safe Rust

Track Q: Lock-Free Flat Hash Page Cache (C SQLite pcache1.c pattern)
Track R: Zero-Alloc Record Encoding (C SQLite OP_MakeRecord pattern)
Track S: Union-Style Register File (C SQLite Mem struct pattern)
Track T: Full BTREE_APPEND Parity (C SQLite btree.c:9394 pattern)
Track U: Dirty-Page Bitmap (C SQLite sqlite3PagerWrite pattern)
","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:30:03Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-e6hvd","title":"F3: Eliminate TransactionHandle Box allocation — stack-allocate via enum dispatch","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:24:47.606348835Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T02:45:26.335857921Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T02:45:26.335548692Z","close_reason":"Committed in 9abedc20: TransactionHandle enum in traits.rs (+213 lines)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":3365,"issue_id":"bd-e6hvd","author":"ubuntu","text":"## What
Replace Box<dyn TransactionHandle> with an enum TransactionKind { Memory(MemTxnHandle), File(FileTxnHandle) }. Stack-allocated, no heap allocation per begin().

## Why
Every statement does begin() which allocates Box<dyn TransactionHandle> (~50 ns). For autocommit with 10K rows, that's 500 µs of pure allocation overhead.

## Where
- crates/fsqlite-pager/src/pager.rs: TransactionHandle trait → TransactionKind enum
- crates/fsqlite-pager/src/traits.rs: update trait bounds
- All callers in connection.rs

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Zero heap allocations in begin()/commit() for :memory: mode
2. cargo test -p fsqlite-pager passes
3. No performance regression for file-backed mode
","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:24:47Z"},{"id":3413,"issue_id":"bd-e6hvd","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Risk & Fallback
**Risk**: The TransactionKind enum may be larger than expected, causing stack pressure.
**Mitigation**: Use `std::mem::size_of::<TransactionKind>()` assertion: must be ≤ 128 bytes. If larger, use SmallBox<dyn TransactionHandle, 128> from smallbox crate instead.
**Fallback**: If enum dispatch causes too many code changes, keep Box but add a per-connection pool of 4 pre-allocated handles (reuse instead of malloc/free each time).
","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:56:14Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-e77h7","title":"Epic: Phase 6 - MVCC Engine & Concurrency Coordination","description":"The core innovation of FrankenSQLite: replace the single WAL_WRITE_LOCK with page-level MVCC. Implement Version Chains, Snapshot Tracking, Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) via the SireadTable, the Safe Write Merge Ladder for resolving conflicts via intent replay, and multi-process shared memory coordination (-shm). E2E concurrent stress testing is mandatory.","acceptance_criteria":"1) Page-level MVCC is the effective concurrency model in runtime (writers do not serialize on WAL_WRITE_LOCK-style global lock).
2) SSI conflict detection/validation and shared-memory coordination paths are integrated and verified under deterministic concurrent stress workloads.
3) BEGIN default behavior remains concurrent-mode ON unless explicitly disabled, with anti-regression coverage across core + e2e harness settings.
4) Epic closure requires deterministic unit/property/e2e evidence and structured traces for version visibility, conflict handling, retry/error semantics, and GC horizon progression.

Cross-cutting quality contract (mandatory):
- Comprehensive unit and property tests covering happy-path, boundary, adversarial, and error conditions with deterministic fixtures and fixed seeds.
- Deterministic e2e scenario scripts with one-command replay instructions and artifact bundle capture.
- Structured logging and metrics with trace_id, run_id, scenario_id, seed, timing fields, outcome, and first-failure diagnostics; CI must validate schema conformance.
- Evidence bundle links in bead notes: executed commands, artifact hashes, and explicit pass/fail summary.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-24T07:06:10.676314564Z","created_by":"unknown","updated_at":"2026-02-26T08:10:01.345395113Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["critical-path","phase6-mvcc"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-e77h7","depends_on_id":"bd-1r0ha","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-e77h7","depends_on_id":"bd-2l5jk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
